turns.user_message: I smoke about 15 cigarettes a day. And I drink on weekends. When I drink, the smoking goes up a lot. My wife has been asking me to stop both.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal

step2_rag_chunks: Alcohol can increase the urge to smoke, so reducing alcohol consumption may help prevent cravings.
Making a daily plan for managing cravings can make the quitting process easier.
Drinking water frequently can help reduce cravings and keep the mouth busy.

step2_rag_chunks: Alcohol can increase the urge to smoke, so reducing alcohol consumption may help prevent cravings.
Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.

step2_rag_chunks: It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
Keeping a diary of triggers and cravings can help identify patterns and develop better coping strategies.
Making a daily plan for managing cravings can make the quitting process easier.
